title: Short-Term Note Payable — Full Cycle
event: On November 1, Reyes Co. borrows $30,000 on a 6-month, 8% note. Prepare the issuance entry, the December 31 interest accrual, and the maturity payment entry on May 1.

lines.label: proceeds
lines.trap: Face value in, face value owed — interest accrues with time, not at signing.

lines.label: obligation
lines.trap: No interest liability yet — zero days have passed.

lines.label: Nov + Dec cost
lines.why: Two months of borrowing cost belong to this year.
lines.trap: The fraction is 2/12 (Nov–Dec) — not the note's full 6-month term.

lines.label: owed, unpaid
lines.why: Accrued but not due until maturity.
lines.trap: This payable is exactly what maturity's entry will pick up first.

lines.label: principal off
lines.why: The face comes off the books.
lines.trap: Principal never touches interest expense — keep the streams separate.

lines.label: last year's 2 months
lines.trap: Without this line, December's interest gets expensed TWICE.

lines.label: this year's 4 months
lines.why: Jan–Apr's interest belongs to the new year: 30,000 × 8% × 4/12.
lines.trap: The 1,200 total interest splits by calendar: 400 last year, 800 this year.

lines.label: maturity value
lines.why: Principal plus all six months of interest.
lines.trap: Maturity value = 30,000 + 1,200, regardless of when year-end fell.

memorize.body: Interest = principal × rate × months/12. Total interest splits between years by the calendar.
The maturity entry has THREE debits when a year-end intervened — payable, expense, principal. Two debits means someone's interest got lost.

questions.prompt: $30,000 note at 8%, signed Nov 1. Interest accrued at Dec 31 = ?

distractors.feedback: That's the full 6-month interest. Only Nov–Dec (2 months) had passed: 1,200 × 2/6 = 400.
A full YEAR at 8%. This note accrued only 2 of 12 months by year-end: 400.

build.scaffold: Borrow at face. Accrue by calendar. At maturity: payable + current expense + principal, one cash credit.
